narration.default: Sometimes inside a loop you want to STOP the whole loop EARLY (before the condition naturally becomes false), or SKIP the rest of the current pass and jump to the next one. C gives you two special keywords for these: break (exit the loop completely) and continue (skip to the next pass). Scratch had "stop this script" inside a forever block — that's like break. Today you'll see both used together: break out at i=5, and skip even numbers with continue.

Lines 5 to 7: if (i == 5) { break; }. The moment i is 5, break fires and the WHOLE loop exits — the rest of the loop body is skipped, and so are all future iterations. Useful for "I found what I was looking for, no need to keep searching" — like checking 100 names for a match and stopping the moment you find one. Without break, you'd either keep looking after success or have to add complicated condition tracking.

steps.title: continue skips to the next pass

narration.default: Lines 8 to 10: if (i % 2 == 0) { continue; }. Remember % from the arithmetic chapter? i % 2 is 0 for even numbers and 1 for odd. So this if reads "if i is even". When continue fires, C SKIPS the rest of the loop body for THIS pass and jumps straight to the next iteration — the printf below NEVER runs for even values. Use continue when you want to SKIP certain inputs but keep going.

Trace pass by pass. i=1: not 5, odd → print 1. i=2: not 5, even → continue (skip print). i=3: not 5, odd → print 3. i=4: not 5, even → continue. i=5: equals 5 → BREAK → loop ends. Click Run. The Console shows just two numbers: 1 and 3. break and continue together let you express "skip these, stop at that" in one short loop — a powerful pattern.
